entries.title: Finding the Sea: Tools That Made the Ocean Measurable
entries.rawOfficialText:
In the fifteenth century, Europeans launched their caravels and galleons on what would become a merciless colonial crusade around the globe. They relied on a Chinese invention, the compass, which transformed the ocean from a terrifying void into a measurable grid. Learn about other key innovations that helped these smallpox-toting explorers navigate the seas, then discuss with your team: what would the world be like today if Europeans had simply stayed home?
astrolabe | sextant | log tables and ephemerides | magnetic compass
the log and line | chronometer | latitude and longitude
entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The National Geographic compass link is central, not optional: it explains both the mechanics and the historical consequences of the compass. It makes clear that a compass indicates direction by aligning a magnetized needle with Earth’s magnetic field, but it also emphasizes complications such as magnetic declination, deviation caused by ship materials, and the later rise of gyrocompasses, solar compasses, and GPS.
The strongest content upgrade is the article’s impact section: the compass helped long-distance exploration and trade, but it also helped colonization, disease, enslavement, and the remaking of global relationships. That matches the raw text’s phrase “merciless colonial crusade” and keeps the entry morally serious rather than treating navigation tools as neutral gadgets.
entries.studentExplanation:
European ocean expansion depended on instruments that turned the sea into something measurable: direction, speed, time, position, and coordinates. The compass link matters because it shows that the compass was not just a needle pointing north; sailors also had to understand magnetic variation, ship interference, and how navigation tools changed the world. These tools made journeys more reliable, but they also helped power trade, empire, disease, and conquest, so progress in navigation was never politically neutral.

The added notes clarify what each tool makes measurable: the compass gives direction, the astrolabe and sextant measure celestial angles, log tables and ephemerides make calculations possible, the log and line estimates speed, and the chronometer helps solve longitude. Navigation is therefore a system of measurements, not one magic instrument. Use this to track movement beyond simple maps: what is guiding the traveler, where the threshold or border sits, how tools, animals, memory, or law shape arrival, and why being near home is not always the same as belonging.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows that leaving home across the ocean required tools, measurements, and learned systems. At the section level, it opens Home and Wandering by asking how humans convert frightening unknown space into routes. At the year-theme level, it connects to “Are We There Yet?” because knowing where you are can create power, but that power can be used for exploration, commerce, domination, or survival.
entries.takeaway: Navigation technology can make unknown space measurable, but it can also expand power and empire.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether navigation tools should be seen as neutral progress or as technologies that helped produce colonial power.
entries.counterargument: Navigation tools can enable harm, but they can also support rescue, trade, scientific knowledge, and safer movement.

visualSections.title: Measuring the sea with sky and magnetism

items.title: Astrolabe
items.note: Astrolabe is presented as an older sky-measuring instrument that helped sailors estimate latitude by observing celestial bodies.
items.imageGuidance: Find a very realistic image of a historical mariner’s astrolabe, preferably from a museum, maritime collection, or educational archive. The image should show the instrument clearly enough that students see it as a measuring device, not decoration. The agent must use /imagesearch first and find the exact named work, object, photo, page image, screenshot, patent, map, or source image. Do not replace it with a generic image. Do not use /imagecreator for an exact source unless the app needs a temporary placeholder and the placeholder is clearly marked as non-final. The image must not be anime; when a realistic reconstruction is unavoidable, it must be very realistic and inspired by the app UI, WSC app mandatory.
items.videoGuidance: Find a short educational video explaining how an astrolabe works for navigation, ideally with a simple demonstration. The agent must use /youtubesearch. Find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, suitable for young teenagers. The video should explain the exact concept, work, or example, not just provide vague background or entertainment.

items.title: Sextant
items.note: Sextant is presented as a more precise angle-measuring tool used to connect the horizon, the sky, and a ship’s position.
items.imageGuidance: Find a very realistic image of a nautical sextant, preferably in use at sea or displayed by a maritime museum. The instrument should be visibly recognizable. The agent must use /imagesearch first and find the exact named work, object, photo, page image, screenshot, patent, map, or source image. Do not replace it with a generic image. Do not use /imagecreator for an exact source unless the app needs a temporary placeholder and the placeholder is clearly marked as non-final. items.videoGuidance: Find a short educational video showing how a sextant measures angles and helps estimate position at sea. The agent must use /youtubesearch. Find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, suitable for young teenagers. The video should explain the exact concept, work, or example, not just provide vague background or entertainment.

Find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, suitable for young teenagers. The video should explain the exact concept, work, or example, not just provide vague background or entertainment.

entries.title: How Animals Find Their Way
entries.rawOfficialText:
We have Google Maps, or Baidu, but how do other animals know when they’ve arrived? From the monarch butterfly’s multigenerational migration to the globe-trotting of the humpback whale, animals follow astoundingly complex routes around the Earth. Learn more about their migration patterns and processes through the terms and examples below, then consider: are animals truly navigating, or just following their instincts? And is human activity making it harder for them to find their way?
magnetoreception | cryptochromes | olfactory navigation | echolocation | zugunruhe
constant frequency | frequency modulation | sunlight polarization | spatial memory
indigo bunting | dung beetle | honeybees | homing pigeons | sea turtles | desert ants

links.label: Dung beetles navigate by the Milky Way
Sea turtles and geomagnetic landmarks - NSF

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The animal links make this entry much richer than a list of terms. The indigo bunting link emphasizes star learning: young birds appear to learn the rotating night sky and use it for migration. The sea turtle NSF link is especially important because it explains a “magnetic map”: turtles can detect differences in Earth’s magnetic field and orient toward feeding or nesting areas, not simply wander by instinct.
The honeybee and desert-ant examples add a useful contrast: animals can use landmarks, path integration, memory, polarized light, smell, sound, or magnetic cues depending on species and environment. The human-impact angle should not be generic; students should see that artificial light, noise, habitat change, pollution, buildings, undersea cables, and disrupted smells can interfere with the signals animals use to move.
entries.studentExplanation:
Animals navigate through many overlapping systems: magnetic sensing, stars, polarized light, smells, echoes, landmarks, spatial memory, and restless migration behavior. The links are important because each example shows a different kind of “map”: indigo buntings use the night sky, sea turtles use magnetic patterns, bees and ants use landmarks and path integration, and pigeons combine several cues. This makes the debate sharper: migration may be instinctive, but it can also involve sophisticated orientation that human activity can disrupt.

Use animal navigation as a set of different “maps”: birds can use stars or magnetism, turtles read magnetic signatures, bees follow landmarks and polarization, bats use echoes, and migratory restlessness changes behavior before the journey begins. The point is that a route can be stored in bodies, senses, and seasons. Use this to track movement beyond simple maps: what is guiding the traveler, where the threshold or border sits, how tools, animals, memory, or law shape arrival, and why being near home is not always the same as belonging.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows students that navigation is not only a human invention. At the section level, it expands Home and Wandering from ships and maps to living bodies moving across the planet. At the year-theme level, it connects to home because many animals somehow leave, wander, and return with stunning precision.
entries.takeaway: Animals navigate through body, environment, memory, and signals that human activity can disrupt.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether animal navigation is instinctive programming or a flexible intelligence shaped by environment and memory.
entries.counterargument: Animal navigation may not match human planning, so calling it navigation can risk projecting human concepts onto other species.

items.title: Magnetoreception
items.note: Magnetoreception is presented as the ability to sense Earth’s magnetic field for orientation.
items.imageGuidance: Find a realistic educational image or diagram showing an animal sensing Earth’s magnetic field, ideally birds or sea turtles with magnetic field lines. Avoid cartoon animal GPS visuals. The agent must use /imagesearch first. The image should be very realistic, not anime, and visually compatible with the beige/brown/maroon WSC app UI. If /imagesearch cannot find a perfect image and this slide is not about an exact artwork, known historical photo, poster, building, patent, object, screenshot, or named visual source, the agent should use /imagecreator to create a very realistic concept image inspired by the app UI, WSC app mandatory.

Find a clear educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, suitable for young teenagers. The video should explain the exact concept, work, or example, not just provide vague background or entertainment.

entries.title: Wayfinding Without Screens
entries.rawOfficialText: “We know where we are,” sang Moana’s ancestors—and they knew it without maps, compasses, or access to Starlink. Instead, Polynesians drew on a complex system of non-instrument navigation, including the star compass, ocean swells, and animal sightings. In the Arctic, the Inuit use the night sky and other markers to find their way—even in a landscape almost uniformly white with ice and snow. With your team, explore traditional navigation methods (including some that may be legendary) and consider: how does the journey change if the map is not on our screen but in our mind? Would the world be better off if more people knew how to navigate it without their phones?

links.label: Did Vikings navigate by polarized light? - Scientific American

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Hōkūleʻa link is the core content source for Polynesian non-instrument navigation: it grounds the entry in real wayfinding systems such as star compass knowledge, swell patterns, wind, clouds, birds, and careful memory. The Moana link is useful as an accessible cultural doorway, but the Hōkūleʻa page should carry the explanation so the app does not treat Disney as the authority.
The Arctic Wayfinders article is extremely rich and should shape the slideshow. It explains Inuit navigation as learned environmental literacy: snowdrifts, currents, sounds, animal movement, atmospheric effects, cairns/inuksuit, oral knowledge, sketch maps, and driftwood/tactile maps. The Viking link is different: it should be framed as possible/legendary navigation, not as fully settled fact.
entries.studentExplanation:
Wayfinding without screens is not guessing. Polynesian navigators read stars, swells, winds, birds, clouds, and memory across huge ocean distances, while Inuit travelers read snow, ice, sound, currents, cairns, animal movement, and story-linked landmarks in the Arctic. The links matter because they show navigation as a relationship with the world: the map can live in the body, memory, elders, and environment, not only on a phone.

The supplemental sources make traditional navigation feel practical rather than mystical: Polynesian navigators read stars, swells, winds, birds, and clouds; Inuit travelers used memory, landmarks, snow, and local knowledge; Viking sunstone stories point to polarized light. No-screen navigation is still data, just not a phone map. Use this to track movement beyond simple maps: what is guiding the traveler, where the threshold or border sits, how tools, animals, memory, or law shape arrival, and why being near home is not always the same as belonging.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students respect traditional navigation as skilled knowledge rather than “primitive” travel. At the section level, it deepens Home and Wandering by contrasting device-based navigation with attention-based navigation. At the year-theme level, it asks whether “getting there” changes when the route is something you know, feel, and remember rather than something an app displays.
entries.takeaway: Wayfinding can live in memory, story, body, sky, ice, wind, and practiced attention.
examples: Polynesian star compass and ocean swells
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether technological navigation improves human freedom or weakens older forms of attention and place-knowledge.
entries.counterargument: Digital tools can expand access and safety, especially for people who lack inherited local knowledge or expert training.

entries.title: Should Some Parts of the World Stay Foggy?
entries.rawOfficialText: In some video games, such as Zelda and Yakuza, the unexplored regions of the map are cloudy until you visit them. The real world used to be more like that: Columbus sailed into the unknown (and never reached his intended “there”) and you couldn’t know what was around the riverbend until you went around the riverbend. Some mapping apps now offer the chance to recreate that experience by masking regions of the map until you set foot in them. Check out one example, Fog of World, which promises us the chance to “experience a richer life”, then discuss with your team: can limiting easy access to knowledge really enrich our experience of the world? If so, what else should we keep “foggy”?
entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Fog of World link is not just a software reference; it supplies the central emotional claim. The app sells itself as a way to “experience a richer life” by hiding unexplored places and turning the world into something gradually revealed. That means the entry is not anti-map; it asks whether full information can flatten curiosity.
The game references matter because they show how fog-of-war mechanics make exploration feel earned. The key classroom idea is a trade-off: easy knowledge increases safety and efficiency, but limited knowledge can increase attention, surprise, memory, and personal investment.
entries.studentExplanation:
Fog of World turns real travel into something closer to game exploration: places stay hidden until you physically visit them. The link matters because the app does not promise better efficiency; it promises a richer experience. That raises the entry’s real question: do maps help us by reducing uncertainty, or can too much certainty make the world feel pre-spoiled before we arrive?

Connect the game idea to real experience: fog of war and Fog of World make ignorance visible, which can make exploration feel meaningful. If every route is already revealed, students should ask whether safety and efficiency improve, and whether discovery, risk, privacy, or wonder are reduced. Use this to track movement beyond simple maps: what is guiding the traveler, where the threshold or border sits, how tools, animals, memory, or law shape arrival, and why being near home is not always the same as belonging.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this asks whether knowledge always improves experience. At the section level, it shifts Home and Wandering from navigation accuracy to the feeling of discovery. At the year-theme level, it connects to “Are We There Yet?” because sometimes the route becomes meaningful precisely because we cannot see the whole destination in advance.
entries.takeaway: Perfect maps can make travel efficient, but they can also shrink mystery, discovery, and wandering.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether the world should be fully mapped, tracked, and knowable, or whether uncertainty has value.
entries.counterargument: Fog and uncertainty can be meaningful, but lack of information can also be dangerous, exclusionary, or inefficient.

entries.title: Stuck in Transit
entries.rawOfficialText: In the 2004 film The Terminal, a traveler lands at an American airport only to discover that his country’s government has collapsed. With his passport no longer valid, he can’t enter the United States, but he also can’t go home again—so he ends up living at the airport indefinitely. Watch highlights from the film with your team, then consider the real case of Mehran Karimi Nasser that inspired it. Why do you think Steven Spielberg made the changes to the story that he did? And what should governments do in situations like these? You may also want to look into some more recent examples of people stuck in airports, including that of Edward Snowden.

links.label: Mehran Karimi Nasseri / true story support

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The film link gives students the accessible version: an airport designed for movement becomes a home-like prison. But the real Nasseri case is essential because the story is not only quirky; it is about documents, asylum, statelessness, mental strain, and systems that do not know what to do with someone between legal categories.
The Reuters Snowden link is a strong political comparison. Snowden’s airport limbo was shorter, but the article shows the same structure: a transit zone becomes a no-man’s-land while passports, asylum requests, diplomatic pressure, and state power decide whether a person can move. The entry should show airports as spaces where legal identity can break down.
entries.studentExplanation:
The Terminal turns an airport into a home, prison, and border all at once: a traveler can move around the terminal but cannot truly enter or leave. The real case of Mehran Karimi Nasseri and the Reuters story about Edward Snowden make the idea more serious. Airport limbo is not just inconvenience; it can happen when passports, asylum, state collapse, and international pressure trap someone between legal systems.

The sources make the airport a legal and emotional threshold: Mehran Karimi Nasseri, Edward Snowden, and The Terminal are not simply “waiting” stories. They show that a person can be physically at a border, gate, or terminal while politically and legally unable to arrive anywhere. Use this to track movement beyond simple maps: what is guiding the traveler, where the threshold or border sits, how tools, animals, memory, or law shape arrival, and why being near home is not always the same as belonging.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows how a place built for movement can become a place of suspension. At the section level, it gives Home and Wandering a clear liminal case: neither home nor destination, neither arrival nor departure. At the year-theme level, it asks what “there” means when a person reaches an airport but still has no place where they fully belong.
entries.takeaway: A transit space can become a suspended home when law, paperwork, and politics block arrival.
examples: The Terminal: airport as suspended home
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether legal categories can turn a journey into an indefinite state of suspension.
entries.counterargument: Airport limbo is dramatic, but many cases depend on rare legal circumstances rather than ordinary travel experience.

entries.title: Reaching the Border Is Not the Same as Being Accepted
entries.rawOfficialText: Immigrants by land and sea can also face obstacles to entry when they reach their destinations. Consider the case of the St. Louis, a ship carrying Jewish refugees from Nazi Germany in 1939. The passengers were denied entry in the United States, Canada, and Cuba, and many ultimately died in Nazi concentration camps. More recently, the United States has stopped allowing people to ask for asylum at its southern border; European countries are also increasingly reluctant to accept refugees. Explore the reasons behind these anti-immigrant policies; do some make more sense than others? Then, discuss with your team: how open should countries be to those arriving at their borders in dire need? Should there be limits on the number admitted, and, if so, how should those limits be calculated?

links.label: The St. Louis refugee ship - HISTORY

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The St. Louis link is the moral center of the entry. It turns border policy from an abstract debate into a concrete historical tragedy: Jewish refugees fleeing Nazi Germany reached the Americas, were denied entry by multiple countries, and many later died after being forced back into Europe. The point for students is that arrival can fail at the threshold.
The modern-policy side should be handled carefully and neutrally. Anti-immigrant policies can be defended through arguments about capacity, security, law, and political stability, but the St. Louis case warns that border decisions can have life-or-death consequences. The slideshow should make the comparison without pretending all historical and current cases are identical.
entries.studentExplanation:
Arriving at a border is not the same as being welcomed through it. The St. Louis case shows this in the most tragic way: people fleeing Nazi Germany reached possible safety, but the United States, Canada, and Cuba refused them, and many later died in the Holocaust. Modern asylum and refugee debates are different in context, but they raise the same threshold question: when people arrive in danger, how do states balance law, capacity, fear, responsibility, and human need?

The St. Louis and migration poems show that arrival has two layers: reaching a place and being recognized by that place. Borders are not just lines on maps; they are decisions made by states, publics, documents, ships, ports, and the stories countries tell about welcome. Use this to track movement beyond simple maps: what is guiding the traveler, where the threshold or border sits, how tools, animals, memory, or law shape arrival, and why being near home is not always the same as belonging.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows that borders are decisions, not just map lines. At the section level, it deepens Home and Wandering by showing that wandering can end at a closed threshold. At the year-theme level, it asks whether “getting there” matters if the destination refuses to let you belong.
entries.takeaway: A border is not only a line on a map; it is a moral threshold that decides who may enter.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether borders mainly protect communities or create unjust exclusions at the threshold.
entries.counterargument: Borders can be cruel, but states also argue that they need rules to manage security, resources, and legal responsibility.

entries.title: Poems of Migration and In-Between Lives
entries.rawOfficialText:
Consider the following poems that speak to the immigrant experience. Are they expressing something universal, or are they too constrained by the specific experience of migrating to the United States? In what ways do they relate to the idea of thresholds and liminal spaces?
Emma Lazarus | “The New Colossus” (1883)
Adrienne Rich | “Prospective Immigrants Please Note” (1963)
Marilyn Chin | “We Are Americans Now, We Live in the Tundra” (1987)
Adrian Castro | “The Sound of One Immigrant Clapping” (2005)
Richard Blanco | “Mother Country” (2019)

links.label: Marilyn Chin - “We Are Americans Now, We Live in the Tundra”
Adrian Castro - “The Sound of One Immigrant Clapping”

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
These poem links should not be treated as decoration; each gives a different angle on migration. “The New Colossus” turns immigration into a symbol of welcome attached to the Statue of Liberty. Adrienne Rich’s poem is almost a threshold manual: crossing and not crossing both change the person. Marilyn Chin’s poem complicates becoming American through ancestry, environment, irony, and identity.
Adrian Castro’s poem works through sound, music, language, and Caribbean memory; it asks how an immigrant voice survives displacement. Richard Blanco’s “Mother Country” moves through family memory and exile, making migration intergenerational rather than only personal. The slideshow should therefore compare emotional functions: welcome, warning, transformation, language, and inherited memory.
entries.studentExplanation:
These poems show migration from the inside: hope, warning, memory, ancestry, language, loss, and the strange feeling of being between homes. The links matter because each poem gives a different version of the threshold. Lazarus imagines welcome, Rich warns that crossing changes you, Chin explores becoming American while carrying older histories, Castro focuses on voice and language, and Blanco turns exile into family memory. Together they show that migration is not only movement; it is a transformation of identity.

Read the poems as different emotional geographies. Lazarus imagines welcome, Rich warns that every choice has risk, Chin uses animals and tundra to show displacement, Castro turns identity into rhythm, and Blanco shows how memory travels with ordinary objects from the old home. Use this to track movement beyond simple maps: what is guiding the traveler, where the threshold or border sits, how tools, animals, memory, or law shape arrival, and why being near home is not always the same as belonging.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students see how poetry can hold complicated migration feelings in a small space. At the section level, it gives Home and Wandering an emotional and literary voice after the policy and navigation entries. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds because migrants may cross borders physically while still living between languages, memories, and homes.
entries.takeaway: Migration poetry turns movement, exile, welcome, and belonging into felt human experience.
examples: “We Are Americans Now, We Live in the Tundra” (1987)
“Prospective Immigrants Please Note”: the door as choice
“We Are Americans Now, We Live in the Tundra”: identity in a new landscape
“The Sound of One Immigrant Clapping”: voice and rhythm
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating how literature can reveal the emotional truth of migration better than policy language.
entries.counterargument: Poems can deepen empathy, but they cannot replace the legal, economic, and political analysis migration also requires.

entries.title: Art, Music, and the Migrant In-Between
entries.rawOfficialText:
Some artists, too, explore the liminality of the migrant. Whether between home and the unfamiliar, or legal and “alien” status, how does each of them treat the state of being in-between?
Frida Kahlo | The Bus (1929)
Jacob Lawrence | Migration (1941)
Led Zeppelin | “Immigrant Song” (1970)
Fiddler on the Roof | “Far From the Home I Love” (1971)
Genesis | “Illegal Alien” (1983)
Brendan Graham | “Isle of Hope, Isle of Tears” (1995)
Kovi Konowiecki | Borderlands (2016)
Los Tigres del Norte | “Somos Mas Americanos” (2013)

links.label: Brendan Graham - “Isle of Hope, Isle of Tears”
Los Tigres del Norte - “Somos Mas Americanos”

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The visual-art links matter because they turn migration and in-betweenness into scenes. Kahlo’s The Bus is not only transport; it places social classes and identities side by side in a moving public threshold. Lawrence’s Migration Series is the anchor: its 60 panels turn mass movement into a visual narrative of departure, labor, hope, crowding, and collective transformation.
The music links widen the emotional range. “Immigrant Song” uses mythic voyage energy; “Far From the Home I Love” makes leaving home intimate and painful; “Illegal Alien” is historically useful but should be handled carefully because its tone and stereotypes can feel dated or offensive; “Isle of Hope, Isle of Tears” turns Ellis Island into memory and hope; “Somos Mas Americanos” is political identity and protest, challenging who gets called American. Borderlands adds a contemporary photographic language of people living near literal and symbolic borders.
entries.studentExplanation:
These works show the migrant in-between through images, panels, rhythm, theater, protest, and memory. Jacob Lawrence’s Migration Series is the strongest visual anchor because it turns mass movement into a 60-panel story of collective displacement and hope. The songs add emotional range: leaving home, crossing borders, being labeled “alien,” remembering Ellis Island, or insisting that identity cannot be reduced to paperwork. Together, the works show that migration is not only a policy topic; it is something people see, hear, perform, and carry in their bodies.

The art and music add the sensory layer of migration: Kahlo’s bus compresses social classes and personal trauma, Lawrence turns migration into a sequence, Fiddler makes leaving a village intimate, and songs such as “Somos Mas Americanos” challenge the idea that borders get the final word. Use this to track movement beyond simple maps: what is guiding the traveler, where the threshold or border sits, how tools, animals, memory, or law shape arrival, and why being near home is not always the same as belonging.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students compare how different art forms make migration visible and audible. At the section level, it closes Home and Wandering by moving from routes and policies to identity and expression. At the year-theme level, it connects to home and liminality because these works ask what it feels like to live between departure and belonging.
entries.takeaway: Art and music can show migrant in-betweenness in ways that policy language cannot.
examples: Fiddler on the Roof, “Far From the Home I Love”
Brendan Graham, “Isle of Hope, Isle of Tears”
Los Tigres del Norte, “Somos Mas Americanos”
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether migrant art represents personal memory, political critique, cultural identity, or all three at once.
entries.counterargument: Art can represent migrant life powerfully, but it can also simplify, aestheticize, or commercialize real displacement.
